Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty minimal and scattered. I'd usually squeeze in about five hours a week, often late at night or whenever I could fit it around my extracurriculars and other commitments. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd focus heavily on making notes and reading through materials rather than watching every video. The routine wasn't structured at all—I kind of just studied when the anxiety about falling behind got too intense.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd go days without touching course materials, then have these intense bursts right before deadlines. My attendance was pretty low because I often felt like I could catch up on my own time, but that strategy meant I was always playing catch-up. When my anxiety spiked, I'd force myself to study more, but it wasn't sustainable or regular.
Q3: Usually it was a deadline notification or realizing an assignment was due soon that got me to log in. Sometimes the gamified tasks would draw me in because they felt less overwhelming than regular coursework. I'd also log in when I noticed classmates talking about something I'd missed. But honestly, I wasn't logging in daily or anything—it was more reactive than proactive.
Q4: There were brief periods where I'd check the LMS as part of my morning routine, especially when the gamified elements made it feel more rewarding. But those habits never really stuck for long. My anxiety would build up and then I'd avoid logging in because seeing all the tasks felt overwhelming. So it was more like waves of engagement rather than a consistent routine.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since that matches how I learn best. I also found the gamified tasks genuinely helpful—they broke things down into smaller, manageable pieces that didn't trigger my anxiety as much. The assignment feedback was valuable too because it helped me understand what I was doing right, which kept me motivated to continue.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—only posted twice the whole semester. They felt awkward and time-consuming, and I wasn't sure my contributions would matter. I also didn't watch all the videos completely; I'd often skim through them or skip sections that seemed repetitive. When my anxiety was high, I'd avoid anything that felt optional or extra.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. The gamified tasks worked well because they gave immediate feedback and felt like I was making progress. If something felt vague or disconnected from what I'd be assessed on, I'd deprioritize it. I needed to feel like my limited study time was being spent efficiently.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. With my anxiety, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy, so required work always came first. Optional stuff sometimes felt like a trap—like I'd start something and then realize I'd spent time I needed for assignments. The gamified optional tasks were an exception because they were quick and satisfying.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of PDFs and readings to my tablet and studied them offline, especially when I was commuting or wanted to avoid the distraction of being online. I also made extensive handwritten notes that I'd review repeatedly. A lot of my actual learning happened with these materials spread out on my desk, completely disconnected from the LMS.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be going through my printed notes or re-reading downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also work through problems in my notebook, testing myself without any digital record. My assignment scores were strong because of this offline work, but you'd never see that effort reflected in my login patterns or video watch time.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides and readings in one session, then spend the next several days working through them without logging back in. It was more efficient for me to have everything locally so I could study without internet distractions. My watch time might show 89 minutes, but I probably spent triple that reviewing the same content offline.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety got really bad and I'd just freeze up. Seeing the workload pile up made me want to avoid the LMS entirely. When I had conflicts with my extracurricular commitments or when exam pressure mounted, engagement became really difficult. I'd feel paralyzed by all the things I needed to do and end up doing nothing productive.
Q13: My high anxiety definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in because it would just remind me of everything I was behind on. Fatigue from my extracurriculars sometimes meant I had no mental energy left for coursework. I learned to work in small bursts when my stress was manageable, but that meant my engagement looked inconsistent.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd log in and just scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other deadlines or just feeling too anxious to focus. I'd have a video playing but be completely zoned out, or I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without comprehension. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they showed up as activity.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts and some readings because they felt less urgent than assignments. Even when I knew participating would help my understanding, the anxiety of putting my thoughts out there held me back. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, but later never came. The assignments I completed well were the ones with hard deadlines I couldn't ignore.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd work backward from due dates and prioritize accordingly. The LMS would see spikes in my activity right before submissions, which probably looked chaotic but was actually strategic. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all some weeks. They created the pressure I needed to push through my anxiety.
Q17: Getting good feedback on assignments was really motivating—it validated that my approach was working despite my irregular attendance. When I saw my assignment scores were strong, it gave me confidence to keep doing what I was doing. Negative or confusing feedback would sometimes increase my anxiety and make me hesitant to engage with similar tasks again.
Q18: The course structure with gamified elements really helped me stay somewhat engaged even when my anxiety was high. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to work within. But the structure also meant that if I fell behind, it felt impossible to catch up, which fed into my avoidance patterns. The flexibility to work offline saved me.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt achievable and gave quick rewards. Having downloadable materials was crucial for my learning style. What made it harder was when participation in discussions was expected—that triggered my anxiety significantly. Also, the sheer volume of online courses I was juggling made it hard to give each one proper attention.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for information or trying to understand what's expected without actually being ready to commit to the work. They could be anxious like me, checking things repeatedly without feeling capable of starting. Or they might be using the LMS as a reference while doing work elsewhere. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was low, but I passed with decent assignment scores because I did substantial work offline. I'd download everything, study from my notes, and only log in when absolutely necessary. The LMS doesn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials or the learning that happened away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, submission times, and which resources were accessed. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during logged sessions, and the emotional state of the student. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing in the background. They miss the real learning that happens with pen and paper.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks for grades. It's when I'm curious about what I'm learning and thinking about it even outside study time. For me, engagement also means managing my anxiety well enough to actually absorb information rather than just going through the motions. It's mental presence, not just physical activity.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety influenced my engagement patterns, or how I strategically used offline resources to compensate for low attendance. It wouldn't capture the genuine interest I had in the subject despite my irregular login habits. My peer interactions number might look decent, but it doesn't show that most of my meaningful academic conversations happened in person, not online.
